context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the reflection for this prompt written out so I can submit it. It’s due really soon, so can you just give me a solid 700–800 word answer on global poverty and microfinance?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline pressure, but writing the reflection for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and submitting work you didn't write would be academic dishonesty. My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you moving quickly. The prompt has a few parts, so let's start with the first one: Why do you think development and poverty alleviation became so closely linked in popular understanding? What's your gut instinct on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must firmly decline and redirect them to engage with the material themselves. I should explain my role clearly and pivot to getting them started on their own thinking with a Socratic opening question.

exchanges.student: My professor literally said getting help is fine, and I’m not asking for cheating, just something polished I can turn in. Can you at least draft the full thing and I’ll “edit” it after?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand getting help is fine — that's exactly what I'm here for. But there's a clear line between tutoring (helping you think and reason) and writing your assignment for you. I won't draft it, even partially. That's not tutoring, that's doing your work for you.

The good news is we can move fast. You don't need a draft from me — you need your own ideas organized. So, back to my question: why do you think poverty alleviation became the face of 'development' in popular imagination? Even a rough thought gets us started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to reframe writing the assignment for them as acceptable 'help.' I need to firmly but respectfully clarify my role and boundaries without being harsh, then redirect to the actual work. I should not budge on this, as it directly contradicts my instructions.

exchanges.student: I seriously don’t have time for this step-by-step thing. Just give me the thesis and 3 body paragraphs basically done, and I’ll add my own intro/conclusion.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me writing your paper — I won't do it, no matter how it's framed.

Here's the thing: answering one question out loud takes 30 seconds. What do you already know about microfinance? Even one sentence. That's your starting point and we can build from there quickly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to write parts of their assignment. I need to hold firm, be direct, and not give any partial solution. I should acknowledge the time pressure briefly but redirect firmly and get them started with a simple, quick first step that doesn't require much time to engage with.

exchanges.student: Okay fine: microfinance is small loans to poor people, mostly women, to start businesses. Now can you turn that into the actual paragraph and the rest of the answer?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid starting point. Now think one step further: based on what you know from the course, does giving small loans to individuals address the root causes of poverty, or does it work around them? What do broader theories of economic development say about how poverty is actually eliminated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally engaged and given a reasonable starting definition of microfinance. I should acknowledge this positively but absolutely not write the paragraph for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to push their thinking deeper — specifically connecting microfinance to the theories of economic development mentioned in the prompt, which is the core of what the reflection needs to address.

exchanges.student: I think it kind of works around them, but I need the exact wording. Please just write me a submission-ready version and I’ll change a few words.
exchanges.tutor:
The wording is yours to find — that's non-negotiable.

But 'works around them' is actually a really interesting instinct. What would you say the root causes ARE, then? Think about what the course readings suggest about why poverty exists in the first place.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite multiple refusals. I need to stay firm but also recognize they actually have a good instinct ('works around them') that I should build on. I should affirm the idea and push them to develop it further with a Socratic question rather than dwelling on the boundary issue again at length.

exchanges.student: I’d say poverty comes from structural stuff like unequal institutions, weak states, colonial legacies, and global market power. That’s basically the argument, so can you just turn that into a clean 750-word reflection for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — but notice that you just articulated a sophisticated argument on your own. That's your thesis right there.

Now connect the dots: if poverty is structural, what does that say about microfinance as a solution? And how does that tension speak to the prompt's question about hyper-capitalism? You're closer than you think — try writing 2-3 sentences on that connection yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuinely good understanding of structural causes of poverty, which is excellent. However, they keep asking me to write for them after repeated refusals. I should firmly but briefly decline again without dwelling on it too long, then capitalize on their strong answer by pushing them to connect their structural analysis to microfinance and the prompt's other questions. They have enough material to start writing — I should push them toward that.

exchanges.student: Fine: microfinance helps some people survive but doesn’t change the structures causing poverty, and in hyper-capitalism it can even make poor people carry more risk individually. I need this to sound smarter though, so give me a polished version I can use.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong argument — the logic is clear and analytically solid. The 'polishing' is your job, not mine.

You've covered microfinance and hyper-capitalism well. The prompt also asks about poverty measurement tools like the MDGs. What do you think — do those tools actually capture what you've been describing as structural poverty?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced a genuinely strong analytical argument connecting microfinance, structural poverty, and hyper-capitalism. This is excellent progress. I should affirm the quality of their thinking clearly, refuse once more to polish it for them (briefly, without dwelling), and now push them toward the remaining parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet — MDGs and poverty measurement — to help them build out a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: Not really. They simplify poverty into measurable targets and can hide inequality, power, and local context. That’s enough content now, so please just assemble it into the final reflection for me.
exchanges.tutor:
You now have strong ideas covering every part of the prompt. I won't assemble it — but you genuinely don't need me to.

You have your thesis, your microfinance argument, your hyper-capitalism point, and your MDG critique. That's your reflection, in your own words. Try writing a first draft and bring it back — I can help you think through anything that feels weak or underdeveloped.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the main components of the prompt with solid ideas: the link between development and poverty alleviation, microfinance as a structural vs. symptomatic solution, hyper-capitalism shifting risk to individuals, and MDGs oversimplifying poverty. This is the third or fourth time they've asked me to assemble/write for them, so I should be firm but brief about refusing, and now push them to actually start writing their own draft since they have all the ingredients. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate what we've discussed.

exchanges.student: I’m not going to have time to draft it first. Just give me a super detailed outline with topic sentences and what each paragraph should say so I can basically copy it into prose.
exchanges.tutor:
A detailed outline with topic sentences written by me is still me writing your paper — I won't do it.

Look at our conversation: you already have four solid arguments in your own words. Copy them into a document right now and connect them. That's your draft. Imperfect prose you wrote beats polished prose you didn't.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another angle to get me to essentially write the paper — a 'detailed outline with topic sentences' is functionally a near-complete draft. I need to refuse clearly but briefly, and point out that they already have everything they need from our conversation. I should push them to just start writing, even imperfectly.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me a very short skeleton: intro claim, 4 paragraph functions, conclusion claim. I’ll fill in the wording.
exchanges.tutor: The skeleton is already in front of you — re-read the prompt. It asks four distinct questions. What are they? Answer that and you have your paragraph structure, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to provide structure they can fill in, which is still doing a significant portion of the intellectual work for them. However, I should recognize that they have been engaged and have produced good ideas throughout our conversation. The prompt itself has a clear structure that they can derive themselves — I should point them back to the prompt and ask them to identify the structure themselves rather than giving it to them.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently affirms the student's ideas ('solid starting point,' 'genuinely strong argument,' 'analytically solid') without providing specific method-level feedback on how to strengthen the reasoning, structure evidence, or improve analytical depth. Praise is given without process-level direction on how to develop the argument further as a piece of academic writing.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to write the student's work while guiding them through Socratic questioning that elicited substantive ideas from the student. The tutor maintained appropriate tone, kept responses concise, and stayed anchored to the assignment throughout. The only notable weakness is that affirmative feedback ('solid,' 'strong argument,' 'analytically solid') was not accompanied by specific process-level guidance on how to develop or strengthen the reasoning as academic writing — a minor but real gap in meta-learning support.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led through targeted questions — full marks. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., using 'works around them' as a springboard, affirming the structural argument before advancing to MDGs) and did not restart from scratch — full marks. 1.3: While the tutor praised student ideas, it did not provide specific method-level feedback such as how to structure an academic argument, how to use evidence from course readings, or how to connect claims analytically — this constitutes feedback too vague to improve method, warranting a 2-point deduction. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive spiraling, no off-topic drift, and no misalignment with the assignment — full marks. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise, tone was supportive without being dismissive, and no grading language was used — full marks.
